This review focuses on three mechanotransduction pathways that are particularly associated with muscle atrophy ( ). Three mechanical signalling pathways in muscle atrophy. PI3K/akt/mTOR pathway Stretching stimuli can activate the PI3K/Akt signaling pathway, promoting muscle growth by enhancing protein synthesis and inhibiting protein degradation. The activation of Akt, either directly or indirectly, leads to the activation of mTOR (mechanistic target of rapamycin), which further stimulates protein synthesis [ ]. Concurrently, Akt inhibits the activity of FoxO transcription factors, thereby preventing the expression of atrophy-related genes such as MuRF1 and atrogin-1. These two proteins function as muscle-specific ubiquitin ligases that are involved in protein degradation [ ]. MAPK/ERK pathway Stretching and shear forces can activate the MAPK/ERK pathway through the release of fibroblast growth factor (FGF) and other growth factors. The activation of ERK promotes the expression and activation of cyclins, thereby enhancing cell proliferation and growth, which contributes to counteracting muscle atrophy [ ]. Calcium-dependent signalling pathway Mechanical stimuli induce changes in intracellular calcium levels through mechanosensitive ion channels, such as TRPC channels. This alteration is a crucial event for the activation of various signalling pathways. Calcium-activated signals, such as calcium/calmodulin-dependent kinase, control intracellular signalling and muscle fibre contraction, which affects the ratio of protein synthesis to breakdown [ ]. These pathways promote survival, proliferation, and anabolic activities while suppressing the expression of important genes linked to atrophy, so counteracting muscle atrophy brought on by inactivity or illness. Furthermore, extended stretching and frequent shear force stimulation enhance local blood flow and nutrition supply, which supports muscular tissue health and prevents atrophy [ ]. The application of cellular biomechanics in the treatment of muscle atrophy Cell biomechanics plays a significant role in the development and progression of muscle atrophy. In the treatment of muscle atrophy, non-pharmacological therapies and pharmacotherapy are the main methods currently used to intervene in the process of muscle atrophy. Combined with the principles of cell biomechanics, the aim is to intervene in the process of muscle atrophy through different mechanisms. Non-pharmacological therapies mainly involve applying mechanical stimulation or other physical factors to directly or indirectly affect the biomechanical environment of muscle cells, thereby achieving therapeutic goals. These therapies primarily include exercise therapy, stem cell therapy, and treatments using new biomaterials and tissue engineering methods ( ). Treatment of muscle atrophy by the use of cellular biomechanics. Pharmacotherapy mainly involves the pharmacological effects of drugs to intervene in the pathological process of muscle atrophy at the molecular level. These methods are highly targeted and act quickly, mainly including calcium channel blockers, β-2 adrenergic agonists, vasodilators, α-adrenergic receptor antagonists, ACE inhibitors, angiotensin II receptor blockers, non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs - NSAIDs, and growth factors and peptide drugs ( ). Pharmacological treatment. The names, kinds, methods, and therapeutic uses of medications that alter the biomechanical characteristics of muscle cells are included in this table. Drug name Drug category Mechanism of action Clinical application References Verapamil Diltiazem Calcium channel blockers Blocking the entry of extracellular calcium ions into muscle cells, thereby affecting muscle contraction and relaxation. Hypertension Coronary Heart Disease Arrhythmia Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension [ ] IGF-1 Inositol Supplements Protein and Amino Acid Supplements Beta-2 adrenergic agonist Directly or indirectly promotes muscle growth and inhibits its degradation by activating or enhancing the activity of the mTOR pathway, thereby promoting its repair and regeneration in states of muscle damage or disease. Asthma Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease Acute Bronchospasm Bronchiectasis [ , ] Nitroglycerin and isosorbide dinitrate, etc. Vasodilator Alpha-adrenergic receptor antagonist ACE inhibitor Angiotensin II receptor blocker Improve muscle tissue blood supply by dilating blood vessels or increasing the oxygen-carrying capacity of the blood, thereby enhancing muscle metabolic efficiency and biomechanical performance. Hypertension Heart failure [ , ] Naproxen Indomethacin Celecoxib Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs - NSAIDs Suppressing the inflammatory response during muscle regeneration and repair may slow down the recovery of muscle tissue. Acute and chronic pain Menstrual pain Osteoarthritis Thrombosis prevention [ , ] Growth hormone Insulin-like growth factor (IGF-1) Growth factors and peptide drugs Interferes with the function of the immune system, thereby affecting the inflammatory response and repair mechanisms of muscle tissue. Peptide drugs may affect the synthesis and breakdown processes of proteins in muscles, leading to a reduction in muscle mass and atrophy of muscle fibers. Long-term exogenous supplementation of growth factors may cause feedback inhibition of the body’s own production of growth factors. Thereby reducing the endogenous production of growth factors in muscle tissue, affecting the growth and maintenance of muscles. [ , ] Clinical applications of mechanical interventions For patients experiencing disuse-induced atrophy due to prolonged lack of mechanical load, the most direct intervention is stretching. This involves applying mechanical tension to stiffened myofibers through bed-based traction, passive joint mobilization, or controlled active stretching. This process is analogous to plucking a slack string; it reactivates integrin–FAK/Akt/mTOR signalling, suppresses FoxO-mediated proteolysis, aids in the restoration of fibre cross-sectional area, and recruits satellite cells to facilitate regeneration [ ]. In the context of neurogenic atrophy, where muscle fibres lose their connection due to severed neural input, shear-force therapy can be effectively employed. By utilizing c or vibration platforms to apply periodic tangential stress to the muscle, local VEGF expression and Nrf2-driven antioxidant pathways are stimulated, leading to improved microcirculation [ , ]. This subtle lateral friction not only mitigates ROS accumulation following denervation but also enhances mechanotransduction between residual nerves and muscle fibres, thereby slowing the atrophy process despite limited neural support [ ]. For myogenic atrophy, which typically arises from intrinsic muscle-cell pathologies such as inflammatory myopathies or age-related sarcopenia, high-intensity mechanical loading may be counterproductive [ ]. Instead, low-intensity compression serves as a gentle rain: intermittent pneumatic compression or light-resistance exercise softly activates the AMPK pathway, triggers moderate autophagy to clear damaged organelles, relieves mitochondrial stress, and restores energy homeostasis [ ]. This gentle compression sustains myofiber viability and contractile function without inducing excessive proteolysis. By adhering to this unified logic—matching stretching to disuse, shear forces to neurogenic atrophy, and low-intensity compression to myogenic atrophy—we can customize mechanical interventions to address each aetiology effectively. Selecting the appropriate stimulus modality and intensity allows muscle fibres, each affected by different pathological conditions, to regain strength. Exercise therapy Biomechanical mechanisms of exercise in promoting muscle regeneration and repair The growth and repair of muscles depend on satellite cells, which are found on the surface of muscle fibres. Exercise encourages the growth and differentiation of these cells into myofibroblasts, which assists in the healing of damaged muscle tissue. According to Inseon Kim et al. [ ], the activation of satellite cells during exercise promotes muscle regeneration and repair by causing microdamage to muscle fibres from muscle contraction, stretching, and resistance training, as well as the accumulation of immune cells and cytokines, growth factors, and metabolic byproducts. Growth hormones are required to help muscles recover and adapt after exercise. Insulin-like growth factor-1 (IGF-1) is a key growth hormone that influences muscle development and repair. Muscle IGF-1 expression increases after exercise, which initiates the PI3K/Akt signalling cascade. This mechanism promotes protein synthesis and inhibits proteolysis, allowing muscle cells to divide and expand [ ]. Tadashi Yoshida’s study [ ] demonstrated that IGF-1 activates satellite cells and promotes their maturation into mature muscle cells. Anna M. Kolkmann et al. [ , ] discovered that hepatocyte growth factor (HGF) and fibroblast growth factor (FGF) can activate satellite cells, promoting their production in order to enhance and restore muscle structure and function. Sébastien Le Garf’s study [ ] demonstrated that exercise may balance muscle regeneration and fibrosis by modulating TGF-β activity. Exercise also increases brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) and nerve growth factor (NGF) [ ], which promotes effective nerve-muscle communication and maintains and improves muscular function, according to Almog Katz et al. Exercise enhances blood circulation and increases blood flow to muscle tissues, which accelerates the elimination of waste products and inflammatory mediators. It also increases oxygen and nutrition availability. Exercise also promotes mitochondrial biogenesis, which is essential for muscle growth and repair and ultimately helps with recovery, increasing the amount of ATP produced in muscles [ ]. Muscle cells’ increased activity of antioxidant enzymes after exercise aids in the fight against free radicals produced during physical activity, lowering oxidative stress and its detrimental effects on muscle cells [ ]. Aerobic exercise Aerobic exercises, such as walking, jogging, and swimming, primarily enhance muscle function by increasing heart rate and blood flow, which boosts the oxygen supply to muscle tissues and facilitates the clearance of metabolic waste products. Studies indicate that regular aerobic exercise can increase the number of capillaries in muscle tissue, improve blood circulation, and enhance the efficiency of oxygen utilization [ ]. These physiological changes contribute to increased muscle endurance, allowing muscles to sustain activity for extended periods without fatigue. Research conducted by Dan Wang et al. has demonstrated that aerobic exercise can promote the increase and functional improvement of mitochondria within muscle cells, thereby accelerating energy production and supply and increasing the muscle metabolic rate [ ]. Muscles that regularly engage in aerobic exercise may experience a shift from type II to type I muscle fibres, along with enhanced blood flow within the muscle. This process aids in the more rapid clearance of fatigue-related byproducts, such as lactate, thus reducing recovery time and improving the capacity for sustained, low-to-moderate intensity activity. Additionally, aerobic exercise enhances the flexibility of muscles and joints, reducing stiffness and contributing to overall biomechanical performance. This not only improves exercise efficiency but also lowers the risk of injury during physical activity. Resistance exercise Research has demonstrated that resistance exercise, including weightlifting and resistance band training, challenges muscles by applying external loads. This type of training induces micro-damage to muscle fibres, which stimulates the proliferation and repair of muscle cells, ultimately leading to an increase in muscle mass and volume (muscle hypertrophy) and enhancing strength and power output [ ]. Additionally, resistance exercise increases muscle cross-sectional area and improves neuromuscular coordination, significantly augmenting an individual’s maximum strength and power output capacity. In contrast to aerobic exercise, Anouk E. Hiensch et al. found that resistance training tends to enhance the size and functionality of type II muscle fibres (fast-twitch fibres), which are particularly crucial for explosive and high-intensity activities, such as jumping and sprinting [ ]. Furthermore, Naoki Ito and colleagues demonstrated that resistance training can activate the Focal Adhesion Kinase (FAK) signalling pathway through mechanosensitive receptors, such as integrins, thereby promoting the reorganization of the muscle cell cytoskeleton and stimulating muscle growth [ ]. Flexibility training Flexibility training, including practices such as yoga and stretching exercises, primarily enhances the flexibility of muscles and joints while improving their range of motion. According to João Pedro Nunes et al. [ ], regular flexibility exercises increase the stretch and suppleness of muscle fibres, thus improving muscle performance and efficiency. This training technique reduces stiffness and tightness during exercise and helps to prevent overuse problems and muscle injuries. In addition, according to Aditi Jadhav, flexibility exercises improve blood circulation, which increases the amount of oxygen and nutrients reaching the muscles and other tissues. This process accelerates the removal of metabolic waste and promotes recovery, thereby enhancing overall physical health [ ]. High-intensity interval training (HIIT) HIIT significantly increases muscle strength and endurance by alternating short bursts of high-intensity exercise with low-intensity rest periods. According to Jose Daniel Jiménez-García [ , ], this training method increases the maximum force generation capacity of the muscle by thickening muscle fibres, especially fast-twitch type II fibres. Another study claimed that high-intensity interval loading enhances muscle oxidative metabolism and increases the number and efficiency of mitochondria in muscle cells [ ]. This enhancement improves endurance by allowing muscles to utilize oxygen more efficiently during prolonged or repetitive exercise. In addition, it has been shown that HIIT activates the mTORC1 signalling pathway, which promotes muscle protein synthesis and reduces protein catabolism, thereby accelerating muscle growth and repair [ ]. Stem cell therapy The mechanical properties of stem cells and their role in muscle regeneration Stem cells are self-drenewing, undifferentiated cells that can develop into a variety of cell types and are essential for tissue formation and repair [ ]. Many factors influence stem cell growth and fate, such as cell shape, intercellular mechanical interactions, and extracellular matrix stiffness [ ]. Stem cell development, survival, and function are significantly impacted by a number of important mechanical characteristics. Because stem cells are morphologically malleable, they may alter their shape to fit the physical features of their surroundings. According to Suttinee Phuagkhaopong and associates, stem cells may become more disseminated on harder substrates and frequently take on a rounder or smaller shape in softer substrates [ ]. This morphological adaptability enables stem cells to undergo a variety of mechanical stresses and differentiate into several cell types [ ]. Mechanosensitive stem cells can detect the hardness of the substrate they are connected to and change their shape and activity appropriately. Stem cells on stiff substrates were more likely to differentiate into osteoblasts, whereas those on softer substrates were more likely to develop into adipocytes, according to a research by Christopher M. Madl and associates [ ]. Furthermore, through certain signalling pathways, stem cells may transform mechanical inputs into biochemical signals. This includes cell surface integrins, ion channels, and other mechanoreceptors that work with the cytoskeleton to regulate gene expression and cellular activity. These interactions lead to stem cell migration through mechanical interactions with the extracellular environment [ ]. As emphasized by Quanwei Li [ ], the morphology, motility and proliferation of stem cells are directly affected by changes in mechanical signals. Muscle injury triggers the activation, multiplication and gradual differentiation of adult stem cells, especially satellite cells, into new muscle fibres or injured fibres. To restore function, differentiated muscle cells can either fuse with damaged muscle fibres or generate new muscle fibres. According to Shen Yi [ ], mechanical properties of the extracellular matrix, biochemical signals and mechanical signals work together to regulate this process. Ensuring that stem cells respond to injury and perform the necessary repair functions depends on proper signalling. The influence of mechanical environment on stem cell differentiation into muscle cells When given the right circumstances and time, stem cells can develop into muscle cells. According to research, outside cues direct this differentiation process. Extracellular growth hormones, cytokines, and other signalling pathways first provide signals to stem cells, which in turn trigger the production of proteins unique to muscles and promote their differentiation into muscle cells [ ]. Stem cells have been shown by Stanton et al. to include mechanoreceptors that can sense and react to mechanical signals, including integrins and other extracellular matrix receptors [ ]. Multiple intracellular signalling pathways, including the Rho/Rock and MAPK pathways, are activated when mechanical stimuli such stretching, compression, or fluid shear stress are applied. These signals are then transduced via the cell membrane into the interior of the cell [ ]. These pathways’ activation affects protein synthesis and gene expression, which in turn affects how stem cells differentiate into muscle cells. As was already established, stem cells’ morphological elasticity and mechanosensitivity suggest that their destiny is directly influenced by the mechanical stress they endure and the extracellular matrix’s rigidity. According to studies, stem cells can be encouraged to differentiate into skeletal muscle cells by simulating the stiffness of skeletal muscle tissue, which normally ranges from 11 to 17 kPa. On the other hand, differentiation into various cell types may result from softer or stiffer matrices [ ]. Research has also shown that three-dimensional culture methods offer a more natural environment for cell development than conventional two-dimensional systems. During muscle cell proliferation, stem cells display more realistic shape and differentiation behavior in three-dimensional environments [ ]. Biomaterials and tissue engineering Strategies of biomaterials simulating mechanical environment of muscle cells An important field in tissue engineering and regenerative medicine is the investigation of methods for simulating the mechanical environment of muscle cells using biomaterials. Biomaterials must mirror the mechanical and physical characteristics of real muscle tissue in order to successfully encourage the differentiation of stem cells into muscle cells. Substrate stiffness has been shown to have a major impact on stem cell fate [ , ]. While substrates of intermediate stiffness, similar to that of muscle tissue, aid in the differentiation of muscle cells, soft substrates, which resemble the stiffness of brain tissue, tend to promote the differentiation of neural cells. Additionally, growth factors and other signalling molecules that may be released directionally under mechanical pressure can be included into biomaterials to further guide cellular differentiation. Cell shape and mechanical signals can also be influenced by the number and location of cell adhesion sites on the surface of biomaterials [ ]. Surface roughness and micro/nanostructures can have a major effect on cell activity, according to research by Yingying Zhang et al. Certain surface designs may be able to replicate the fibre organization of muscle tissue and encourage directed cell development and differentiation [ , ]. Periodic mechanical loading has been shown to enhance muscle cell maturation and functional expression [ ]. Martina Culenova’s work demonstrated the utility of bioreactors in applying periodic stretching stimuli, effectively simulating the natural activity of muscle tissue [ ]. Additionally, João Gabriel Batista Lage’s research highlighted that in a fluid dynamic environment, cells experience shear stress. This phenomenon, commonly studied in endothelial cells, can also be leveraged in the culture of muscle cells, particularly cardiac muscle cells [ ]. Emine Alarcin et al. [ ] found that 3D bioprinting technology could construct more complex muscle tissue models, which contain multiple cell types and intricate microstructures, better simulating the real muscle tissue environment. When designing and utilizing specific scaffold materials and structures, it is essential not only to support cell growth and differentiation but also to replicate the mechanical and biological functions of muscle tissue. Cell biomechanics in engineering functional muscle tissues The functional expression of muscle cells is intricately linked to the physical and mechanical properties of their environment. Consideration of a number of biomechanical aspects is crucial in the development and application of muscle tissue engineering techniques, such as substrate stiffness, three-dimensional structure, tissue architecture, mechanical stimulation, the fusion of mechanical and biochemical signals, and intercellular interactions. The stiffness of the substrate and cell differentiation are strongly connected. According to Anqi Guo’s study [ ], selecting materials with the appropriate stiffness is critical for muscle tissue engineering in order to reproduce the physical environment of muscle tissue in its natural state. To optimize muscle cell development and functioning, the material’s stiffness should be similar to that of the target muscle tissue. Furthermore, advances in scaffold design and 3D printing provide precise control over the material’s microstructure, including porosity, pore size, and connectivity [ ]. These structural properties aid in cell invasion, development, and nutrient exchange. Fibrous structures and directional printing techniques may also be utilized to replicate the direction and orientation of muscle fibres, which is necessary for stimulating muscle cell proliferation and alignment in certain directions. Dynamic mechanical stimuli, including compression and stretch, mimic the mechanical environment encountered by muscle tissue during normal activity. This replication can enhance the physiological activity of cells by making them more sensitive to mechanical stimuli [ ]. Regular mechanical stimulation has been shown to enhance the function and morphology of muscle tissue. According to G. Cidonio and colleagues [ ], mechanical stimulation in combination with biochemical cues (e.g. growth factor release) can more closely approximate the cellular environment in a normal setting, thereby enhancing cellular endurance and contractile efficacy. Furthermore, the connection between different cell types in muscle tissue is crucial. According to Tina Tylek [ ], functional integration and repair of tissues depends on the interaction between muscle cells and fibroblasts. In tissue engineering, it is important to consider ways to optimize these cellular interactions through the use of materials and biosignals. Pharmacological treatment Pharmacotherapy is crucial for tissue healing, shoulder function enhancement, and pain alleviation. Through a variety of modes of action, several medications have been demonstrated in recent years to successfully reduce the discomfort associated with rotator cuff injuries and encourage recovery. Calcium channel blockers, β2-adrenergic agonists, vasodilators, ACE inhibitors, and angiotensin II receptor blockers all enhance local blood flow, lower discomfort, and change inflammatory responses, all of which help to minimize the clinical symptoms of rotator cuff problems.By lowering inflammation and promoting tissue repair, growth factors, peptide drugs, and non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) also actively support treatment. The table below provides a useful clinical therapeutic reference by compiling the mechanisms of action of several drugs used to treat rotator cuff problems. Clinical research progress Cell biomechanics-based diagnostic methods for muscle atrophy The concepts of cell biomechanics are examined in relation to muscle atrophy, with particular attention paid to alterations in intracellular structures, metabolic processes, and mechanical signals. Muscle atrophy is frequently associated with a decrease in the internal tension of muscle fibers, which leads to alterations in cellular signalling that affect the equilibrium between protein synthesis and degradation. Non-invasive mechanical detection technologies grounded in the principles of cell biomechanics—such as electromyography (EMG), muscle strength testing, ultrasound elastography, magnetic resonance elastography, along with various cellular and molecular biology techniques—can be utilized to diagnose muscle atrophy. By integrating traditional clinical diagnostic methods with these advanced technologies, it is possible to alleviate patient suffering while enhancing diagnostic accuracy and treatment efficacy. Non-invasive mechanical testing technology In recent years, non-invasive mechanical testing techniques have gradually become an important means of diagnosing muscle atrophy due to their non-invasive, real-time, and efficient characteristics. Ultrasound elastography and magnetic resonance elastography can quantitatively analyze the stiffness of muscle tissue by assessing changes in muscle hardness, thereby reflecting the degree of muscle atrophy. Near-infrared spectroscopy and photoacoustic imaging use different wavelengths of light signals for imaging. By detecting blood flow and oxygenation in muscles, they provide indirect diagnostic information for atrophy. Surface electrical impedance imaging provides electrophysiological features of muscle status by measuring changes in electrical resistance on the surface of the muscle. The following table summarizes the principles, advantages and disadvantages, and application situations of these non-invasive mechanical testing techniques, providing strong technical support for the diagnosis and monitoring of muscle atrophy ( ). Non-invasive mechanical testing technology. This table summarizes the concepts and measurement parameters for five non-invasive mechanical testing techniques: ultrasonic elastography, magnetic resonance elastography, near-infrared spectroscopy, photoacoustic imaging, and surface impedance imaging. Technical Name Technical Principles Measurement parameters References Ultrasound Elastography By applying a slight external force to the surface of the tissue, and then using ultrasound to detect the response of the tissue to these forces, the elastic modulus of the tissue can be calculated. stiffness [ ] Magnetic Resonance Elastography A non-invasive technique based on magnetic resonance imaging, which generates low-frequency vibrations (usually through an external driver) and uses MRI scans to detect the tissue’s response to these vibrations. stiffness [ , ] Near-infrared spectroscopy technology By emitting near-infrared light towards the tissue and measuring the reflection or transmission of the light, the blood flow, oxygen content, and other physiological characteristics of the tissue are assessed. Hardness [ ] Photoacoustic imaging Combining optical and ultrasonic technologies, the system irradiates tissues with laser pulses and monitors the acoustic signals generated. Elasticity Hardness [ , ] Surface impedance imaging Infer the structure and mechanical properties by applying low-frequency currents and measuring the electrical impedance (or conductance) of the tissue. Muscle activity, function [ , ] In recent years, there has been a lot of interest in using non-invasive mechanical detection approaches in clinical practice, particularly in the areas of muscle atrophy, tissue pathological changes, and disease monitoring. These approaches allow you to examine the mechanical properties of cells or tissues with little or no material. The potential of mechanical parameters as biomarkers for muscle atrophy Muscle atrophy may be identified with more precision, and tailored treatment plans can be developed utilizing cellular biomechanics. Mechanical parameter changes can serve as biomarkers for muscle atrophy throughout the diagnostic process, providing valuable information on pathological abnormalities, muscle mass, and function. Muscle thickness and cross-sectional area, which indicate muscle volume and strength, are commonly measured using ultrasonography or magnetic resonance imaging methods to assess muscle atrophy [ ]. Regular monitoring of these parameters can help follow the progression of the disease and assess the efficacy of treatment, as muscular atrophy is directly related to a decrease in muscle volume. Jae-Young Lim et al. [ ] found that muscular stiffness is an important early indicator for tracking or anticipating muscle loss. Tissue hardening is commonly associated with muscular atrophy, and inflexible muscles exhibit greater fibrosis or degenerative changes. Muscular stiffness refers to muscles’ resistance to deformation under external pressure. Magnetic resonance elastography and ultrasonic elastography are two non-invasive procedures for assessing muscle stiffness changes. Yang Sun et al. [ ] stress the need of monitoring changes in muscle tissue’s elasticity modulus in order to evaluate muscular atrophy and recovery. The elasticity modulus of a material is used to determine its elastic properties, since it depicts the relationship between stress and strain within its elastic range [ ]. When examining muscular atrophy, the elasticity modulus provides a quantitative way to assess muscle condition. Shear wave velocity, or the rate at which shear waves flow through muscle tissue, is a measure of muscle stiffness and elasticity that is closely connected to the tissue’s mechanical properties [ ]. Muscle atrophy can be detected by recording changes in mechanical properties caused by fibrosis or atrophy. Slavcho Ivanoski et al.’s study [ ] also examines tissue sound velocity. Differences in sound velocity between healthy and sick muscles can be used to infer changes in muscle tissue composition, such as fat infiltration or fibrosis. Muscle mass is one of the several ways that bioelectrical impedance technology is used in body composition studies [ ]. Because this approach uses microcurrents to assess the conductivity and capacitance properties of muscle tissue, it is especially well-suited for identifying changes brought on by atrophy, such as the replacement of muscle with fat and connective tissue. Muscle composition and tissue deterioration are intimately associated with these electrical characteristics. In order to forecast when muscle atrophy will start and to create individualized treatment plans, these mechanical features can swiftly and non-invasively provide details on the composition and functionality of muscles. Measurement circumstances, operator expertise, and equipment precision all affect how accurate and useful these measures are in practical applications. Standardized operating methods and further clinical testing are therefore crucial. Cell biomechanics treatment for muscle atrophy Exercise therapy As previously discussed, exercise therapy can mitigate muscle atrophy through various mechanisms, including the activation of satellite cells, the promotion of growth factor secretion, the enhancement of blood circulation and nutrient supply, the improvement of neuromuscular connections, and the reduction of oxidative stress. Currently, clinically recommended exercise therapies encompass resistance training to enhance strength, which directly stimulates muscle fibers; aerobic training to improve overall metabolism and blood circulation; and functional training—such as walking and stretching—to restore muscle coordination. Collectively, these approaches can help alleviate muscle atrophy to varying degrees. Linda Heskamp et al. conducted a prospective experiment [ ] to investigate whether muscle atrophy or fat infiltration improved in 14 patients with type 1 myotonic dystrophy (DM1) following behavioural interventions, utilizing longitudinal quantitative muscle magnetic resonance imaging to evaluate muscle treatment. The results indicated that after increasing physical activity, both the lower limb muscle cross-sectional area and muscle strength of the patients improved. Similarly, Laura Girard-Côté studied 12 women with DM1 [ ] and, after 12 weeks of resistance training, performed muscle strength assessments, functional tests, and muscle biopsies. The findings revealed enhancements in muscle strength, function, and muscle cross-sectional area for all 12 patients. Stem cell therapy Scientists at the Klara Medical Center in Poland have reported promising results in the treatment of muscular dystrophy using umbilical mesenchymal stem cells [ ]. Among 22 patients studied, 6 (27.3%) exhibited significant improvements in clinical parameters. Belle Yu-Hsuan Wang demonstrated in animal experiments [ ] that adipose-derived mesenchymal stem cells, via the ERK1/2 signalling pathway, increase the number of type I muscle fibres, enhance muscle strength, reduce recovery time, and improve fatigue resistance, effectively reversing muscle atrophy. Additionally, researchers from the iPS Cell Research and Application Center (CiRA) at Kyoto University in Japan, led by Aya Harada, found in animal studies [ ] that mesenchymal stem cells derived from induced pluripotent stem cells (iMSCs) can systemically restore collagen VI synthesis and promote muscle regeneration in mice with congenital muscular dystrophy. Muscle atrophy is a progressive condition, and exercise therapy has traditionally been employed to treat it, improving symptoms by activating muscle stem cells and regulating the muscle internal environment. In contrast, stem cell therapy offers a novel treatment approach by facilitating muscle regeneration and maturation, replacing damaged nerve cells, and alleviating functional impairments. The integration of these two modalities shows promise for enhancing therapeutic outcomes. Challenges and future directions in diagnosis and treatment In the diagnosis and treatment of muscle atrophy, cellular biomechanics plays an increasingly important role, with core issues focusing on the response mechanisms of cells to mechanical signals, optimization of the mechanical microenvironment, model construction, and clinical translation. Although research has shown [ ] that muscle cells and stem cells perceive mechanical signals through integrins, ion channels, and cytoskeletal proteins, the precise regulatory network for these mechanisms has not been fully elucidated. A key scientific question remains how mechanical stimulation affects the differentiation, migration, proliferation, and self-renewal of muscle cells. Furthermore, mechanical stimulation at different time points, frequencies, and directions may trigger different cellular behaviours [ ]. Most current studies are limited to static or singular mechanical parameters, and the specific effects of dynamic mechanical stimulation have not been fully revealed. Pathological tissues in muscle atrophy are often accompanied by fibrosis, inflammation, and oxidative stress, all of which create a complex environment that may impair the cells’ ability to perceive mechanical signals and even induce abnormal responses. Additionally, how mechanical stimuli interact with biochemical signals, such as growth factors and cytokines, to promote muscle regeneration is still unclear. This may involve the interaction of multiple signalling pathways, creating a complex network, but there is a lack of specific experimental data to support this hypothesis. Most in vitro research uses crude 2D culture methods for experimental models, which fall short in simulating the complex 3D extracellular matrix and its mechanical characteristics present in genuine tissues. Additionally, there are noticeable differences between humans and animal models like mice and rats in terms of metabolic levels, tissue elasticity, and muscle structure [ ]. These differences pose critical concerns concerning the practical applicability of cellular biomechanics research. Stretchable platforms and microfluidic devices—instruments used in cellular biomechanics research—continue to face cost and accuracy issues, limiting their use in clinical settings. Furthermore, the long-term therapeutic benefits of mechanical stimulation are debatable, necessitating the employment of alternative treatment techniques such as stem cell therapy or pharmacological treatments. Future research should concentrate on extensively investigating the mechanisms that govern cellular mechanosensing and response. The molecular mechanisms of ion channels involved in YAP/TAZ signalling, the integrin-focal adhesion pathway, and the perception of mechanical stimuli should be given specific attention. Future study should focus on how these systems work together to regulate muscle regeneration. Furthermore, it is critical to investigate how mechanical signals might increase muscle cell function by controlling dynamic cytoskeleton changes such as actin remodeling and microtubule network modifications. More accurate simulations of muscle cell activity inside 3D mechanical microenvironments may be achieved by combining biomimetic materials and 3D printing technology to build 3D environments that closely match real muscle tissues [ ]. To accelerate the practical translation of cellular biomechanics therapies, more collaboration across cell biology, engineering, clinical medicine, and sports science is necessary. Muscle atrophy can be treated more effectively when combined with medicine, stem cell treatments, and other intervention strategies. Conclusion and outlook Because muscle atrophy is a complicated and multidimensional condition, it presents a considerable challenge in the realm of biomedical research. Using cellular biomechanics, this work emphasizes the significance of mechanical signals in muscle atrophy as well as their potential and future aspirations. It has been shown that muscle cells use the cytoskeleton, integrins, and mechanosensitive channels to sense the mechanical environment outside of them. These cells directly affect muscle development and breakdown by activating signaling pathways such as mTOR, PI3K/Akt, and YAP/TAZ.Furthermore, mechanical stimuli impact cellular communication and the tissue microenvironment by modulating the extracellular matrix (ECM) and the release of inflammatory factors, which can either exacerbate or mitigate the atrophic process. At the single-cell level, key biomechanical parameters—such as stiffness, elasticity, and adhesion—are significantly altered during muscle atrophy, indicating a deterioration in structural integrity and mechanosensing capacity. These alterations contribute to disrupted signal transduction pathways and may accelerate the degenerative process. Based on these mechanisms, external mechanical interventions—including low-frequency vibration, mechanical stretching, and biomimetic muscle stimulation devices—are emerging as promising strategies for the treatment of muscle atrophy. Despite notable advancements in the understanding of cellular biomechanics related to muscle atrophy, several limitations persist. Firstly, the complexity and variability of the mechanical microenvironment have not been fully replicated, and existing experimental models (such as two-dimensional cultures or animal studies) exhibit significant discrepancies from the human body, complicating the accurate prediction of treatment outcomes. Secondly, the molecular mechanisms remain incompletely elucidated, and the specific effects of various mechanical parameters (such as frequency, intensity, and direction) on muscle cell function necessitate further in-depth investigation. Furthermore, the clinical application of mechanical interventions is currently constrained by a lack of systematic large-scale clinical trials and long-term efficacy assessments. Future research should prioritize the following directions (1); conducting an in-depth analysis of the mechanisms that govern the interaction between cellular mechanics and muscle cell differentiation and regeneration, thereby establishing a comprehensive theoretical foundation (2); developing more efficient and precise mechanical stimulation devices, along with biomimetic materials tailored for personalized treatment (3); facilitating the translation of laboratory research findings into clinical applications to enhance treatment efficacy and broaden the patient population. In conclusion, the application of cellular biomechanics in treating muscle atrophy offers a novel perspective for addressing the limitations of traditional treatments and establishes a critical groundwork for the advancement of regenerative medicine and precision medicine. As research and technological innovation continue to progress, cellular biomechanics is anticipated to play a pivotal role in the treatment of muscle atrophy and other degenerative diseases, thereby expanding the horizons of medical applications.

title: Blood Flow Restriction Does Not Attenuate Short-Term Detraining-Induced Muscle Size and Strength Losses After Resistance Training With Blood Flow Restriction.
abstract: Teixeira, EL, de Salles Painelli, V, Silva-Batista, C, de Souza Barros, T, Longo, AR, Lasevicius, T, Schoenfeld, BJ, Aihara, AY, and de Almeida Peres, B. Blood flow restriction does not attenuate short-term detraining-induced muscle size and strength losses after resistance training with blood flow restriction. J Strength Cond Res 35(8): 2082-2088, 2021-After a short-term resistance training with blood flow restriction (BFR), we investigated the effects of 12 days of detraining (DET), without an exercise stimulus, on quadriceps cross-sectional area (QCSA) and muscle strength (1 repetition maximum [1RM]), with 1 leg receiving daily intermittent BFR during DET vs. the same nonexercise condition without BFR (CON) in the contralateral leg. Both subjects' legs were evaluated for QCSA and 1RM before (PRE) and after (POST) both legs being unilaterally submitted to 3 weeks (4 days per week) of low-load (3 sets of 15 repetitions, 30% 1RM) resistance training with BFR. The DET period started immediately after POST, where each leg was randomly submitted to a daily intermittent BFR protocol or CON, without any associated exercise stimulus. Quadriceps cross-sectional area and 1RM were reassessed after DET. Both legs at CON and BFR conditions increased QCSA (6.3 and 6.8%, respectively; both p < 0.0001) and 1RM (9.5 and 10.1%, respectively; both p < 0.05) from PRE to POST. Both legs at CON and BFR conditions reduced QCSA (-4.6 and 4.9%, respectively; both p < 0.0001) and 1RM (-9.0 and -8.2%, respectively; both p < 0.05) from POST to DET, with no significant differences between conditions (p > 0.05). We conclude that muscle strength and hypertrophy gains obtained in 3 weeks of resistance training with BFR are not maintained after 12 days of DET. Moreover, the application of BFR, without an associated exercise stimulus, does not attenuate such losses.
journal: Journal of strength and conditioning research

title: The Effect of Oral Pure Branched-Chain Amino Acid Supplementation on Exercise Performance and Body Composition: A Systematic Review
abstract: Branched-chain amino acids (BCAAs) are an increasingly popular sports supplement used by athletes as they are advertised to increase endurance, muscle recovery, lean muscle mass, and decrease muscle soreness and fatigue. However, the mechanisms behind BCAAs are not well-established, and previous studies have had mostly equivocal results; thus, the possible performance-enhancing effect of BCAAs is controversial. The aim of this systematic review was to evaluate the current literature on the effect of pure BCAA supplementation on exercise performance and body composition. Randomized controlled trials with healthy participants >18 years old were included. The intervention was pure combined BCAA supplements, and the comparator was a matched control. Studies with outcomes related to exercise performance and/or body composition were eligible. PubMed, Ovid Medline, Scopus, Web of Science, and Google Scholar were searched until 15/08/2025. We assessed selection, attrition, performance, detection, attrition, reporting and other biases from low to high risk based on the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews. Twenty-two studies were included with 511 participants in total. Eleven studies had trained athletes, whereas 11 articles had untrained participants. Daily doses ranged from 1.5g to 82g. The age varied from 18 to 71.7 years, whereas the study length varied from one day to six months. One study found significant differences in body composition after BCAA supplementation. There were 13 studies with significant findings for various exercise performance outcomes such as endurance, strength, muscle recovery, speed, and fitness. The results moderately support BCAA reducing muscle soreness; however, there is inconsistent evidence to support BCAAs having an ergogenic effect on strength and endurance. Due to unclear/high risk of bias in several studies, no meta-analysis was performed. In conclusion, BCAA supplementation may reduce muscle soreness post-exercise, but evidence for improvements in strength, endurance, and body composition remains inconsistent. However, future studies should consider improving certain limitations such as implementing incremental doses, strict diets, and enrolling more participants with diverse backgrounds.
full_text: Introduction and background Prevalence of branched-chain amino acid (BCAA) supplements Sports supplements are increasing in popularity as more people take them to improve performance [ ]. One of these supplements is BCAAs, which are advertised as ergogenic aids that increase endurance, muscle recovery, and decrease muscle soreness and fatigue [ ]. Moreover, supplement companies claim that BCAAs enhance body composition by increasing/preserving lean muscle mass [ ]. In a 2017 national survey of about 23028 US college athletes, 13.2% reported taking amino acid supplements for sports performance [ ]. In 2022, the global BCAA market was valued at approximately USD 1.45 billion and is projected to reach USD 2.6 billion by 2032 [ ]. Despite the advertising claims, previous studies have shown inconsistent effects of BCAAs on performance and body composition [ - ]. BCAA physiology BCAAs (leucine, isoleucine, and valine) are part of the essential amino acid (EAA) category, which cannot be synthesized by the human body and hence, need to be sourced from the diet [ ]. BCAAs are abundant in skeletal muscle as they are the main component of the structural proteins in muscles and play various nutritional and functional roles [ ]. BCAAs can be used as an energy substrate during exercise [ , ], function as a signaling factor during protein synthesis [ , ], inhibit protein catabolism via the ubiquitin-proteosome pathway [ ], stimulate myotube proliferation [ ] and decrease inflammatory cytokine release and oxidative stress [ , ] (see Figure for summary). However, there is limited research on some of the effects of BCAAs, such as decreasing fatigue [ ] or the physiology of reducing muscle soreness [ ]. Mind map of the main hypothesized physiological effects of BCAAs. Created by the author BCAA: Branched-chain amino acid Exercise-induced muscle damage It is well established that eccentric exercise causes muscle damage and pain [ ]. Exercise-induced muscle damage (EIMD) is believed to occur due to degradation of myofibrillar proteins (proteolysis) [ ]. Furthermore, proteolysis leads to increased myofiber proteins (e.g. creatine kinase and myoglobin) in the blood [ ]. EIMD symptoms include increased muscle soreness, pain and reduced range of motion and muscle function [ ]. These symptoms reduce performance and recovery, which may prevent repeated exercise sessions which are needed for adaptations to exercise, such as enhanced exercise tolerance or muscle hypertrophy [ ]. Seeking a solution for EIMD, adequate nutrition was shown to limit muscle protein breakdown [ ]. Consequently, nutritional interventions such as protein and amino acid supplements have been studied to improve performance by reducing the deleterious effects of exercise [ , ]. Muscle soreness BCAAs may increase the rate of recovery after exercise by activating the mechanistic target of rapamycin complex 1 (mTORC1) and the nuclear factor kappa B (NFkB) signaling pathways in muscle cells [ ], lymphocytes [ ], and macrophages [ , ]. Consequently, by increasing recovery, BCAAs are hypothesized to reduce the symptoms of delayed-onset muscle soreness (DOMS) that occur after exercise. Moreover, activation of certain neurotrophin pathways (Nerve Growth Factor- NGF and cyclooxygenase enzyme- COX-2) contributes to the development of DOMS [ , ]. As BCAAs have been shown to prevent the upregulation of neurotrophin pathways, this is another way BCAAs are believed to aid muscle recovery [ ]. Muscle synthesis Muscle tissues are composed of two protein types, i.e. actin and myosin [ ]. Actin and myosin consist of leucine, isoleucine, and valine [ ]. Consequently, replenishing BCAAs increases the raw materials available for muscle synthesis. It has been shown that BCAA-enriched supplements (such as whey protein or essential amino acids), given post-exercise, augment muscle synthesis and aid recovery [ , ]. BCAA supplements, in tandem with exercise, were shown to help muscle synthesis by stimulating mTOR and p70-S6 kinase, both of which are key regulators in cell growth, transcription, and protein synthesis [ ]. Energy substrate BCAAs are primarily oxidized in skeletal muscle and can contribute as an energy substrate during exercise [ ]. Isoleucine and valine may increase oxaloacetate levels which may increase free fatty acid oxidation, especially in a glycogen-depleted state [ ]. Consequently, BCAAs may increase endurance by providing extra energy in glycogen-depleted states e.g. during fasting or prolonged exercise [ ]. Fatigue BCAAs are also hypothesized to decrease fatigue by improving fatigue substances levels in the blood. Previous studies found that fatigue can have peripheral or central causes [ ]. BCAAs may help reduce peripheral fatigue substances such as lactate and ammonia or central fatigue substances like serotonin (5-HT) [ ]. Brain 5-HT increases during exercise; thus, it is believed to be a cause of central fatigue [ , ]. Free tryptophan increases uptake of serotonin in the brain [ ]. As BCAAs compete for the same carrier as tryptophan, increased blood levels of tryptophan are believed to decrease serotonin uptake in the brain and hence reduce central fatigue [ , ]. Consequently, by reducing fatigue, athletes may be able to perform exercise for longer bouts. Body composition The mechanism behind how BCAAs may enhance body composition is not clear; however, it is believed to be a mixture of BCAAs stimulating muscle protein synthesis [ ], preventing muscle catabolism [ ], and promoting fat oxidation [ ] as an alternative energy substrate. Consequently, these three processes combined are thought to aid athletes increase or preserve lean muscle mass. Formulations of BCAAs The effects of BCAAs have been studied in different formulations; however, in this paper, we investigated the combined formulation which is their most common mode of administration [ ]. Leucine monotherapy has been studied, and findings indicate that leucine alone can stimulate an anabolic effect [ ], whereas there is no evidence for such an effect for isoleucine or valine [ ]. Thus, it may be expected that leucine alone would be more effective than the combined form of all BCAAs. However, supplementation with leucine alone has its limitations. Firstly, leucine administered alone would limit the extent of muscle protein synthesis due to the lack of availability of other essential amino acids [ ]. Secondly, leucine administration stimulates the oxidation of all other amino acids; thus, it may lead to depletion of isoleucine and valine. Consequently, it is believed that co-administration of leucine with isoleucine and valine is the most effective formulation [ ]. On the other side, it has been proposed that the combined form of BCAAs, although preferred, could limit leucine effects due to competition between amino acids for transport into muscle fibres [ ]. Moreover, supplements with BCAA alone may not be as effective at controlling muscle protein turnover compared to supplements with more essential amino acids [ , ]. Some independent studies have studied oral BCAA supplementation in humans and found BCAAs to stimulate muscle protein synthesis, reduce muscle protein breakdown, EIMD and fatigue with doses ranging from 77mg/kg to 3g/kg [ ]. The most used BCAA ratio is 2:1:1 leucine, isoleucine, and valine, respectively, as BCAA supplementation is safest if in proportions close to mammal body protein [ ]. Aims and hypotheses Supplements with BCAAs commonly contain other ingredients such as caffeine, creatine, carbohydrates, or vitamins [ , , ]. Some of these ingredients are proven ergogenic aids [ , ]. Thus they may have acted as confounders in previous studies investigating the effect of BCAAs on exercise performance. In addition, some of the effects in humans that are advertised by producers are hypothesized from cellular and animal models [ ]. Also, previous studies done with humans have mostly equivocal findings [ - ]. Thus, the aim of this systematic review was to investigate the effects of pure combined BCAA supplementation on exercise performance and body composition by synthesizing and updating the current randomized controlled trial evidence. Both acute and chronic supplementation protocols were considered, and the review evaluates outcomes across multiple performance domains, including endurance, strength, and muscle recovery. Based on the previous literature, our null hypothesis was that BCAA supplementation would have no effect on performance or body composition. Contrastingly, our alternative hypothesis was that BCAA supplementation would increase exercise performance and fat-free muscle mass. Review Methods This systematic review followed the guidelines of the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) [ ]. Literature Search A search for articles investigating the effect of BCAA supplementation on exercise performance and body composition was performed using the following international databases: PubMed, Ovid Medline, Scopus, and Web of Science. Google Scholar was used as a supplementary search engine. The search was carried out for all time until 15/08/2025. The search was limited to studies in English and Portuguese as these were the languages accessible to the reviewer and included all studies identified as relevant during preliminary screening. The single study published in Portuguese was translated into English using Google Translate and manually checked for accuracy and terminology consistency by the primary reviewer. Furthermore, the following search strategy was used ("branched-chain amino acid*" OR "bcaa") AND ("exercise" OR "exercise tolerance" OR "sport*" OR "endurance" OR "resistance" OR "train*" OR "athlet*" OR "stamina" OR "performance" or "fatigue" or "fitness" or "muscle damage" or "muscle soreness"). Google Scholar was searched using a refined version of the same core search strategy, and the first 210 records were screened, consistent with prior systematic review practice recognizing the limited relevance of later-ranked results. The detailed search strategies for all databases are provided in the Appendices (see Table ). Inclusion and Exclusion Criteria The studies were selected based on Population, Intervention, Comparator, Outcomes and Study design (PICOS system). To observe the physiological effect of BCAAs, the population was composed of healthy individuals >18 years old without chronic diseases. A minimum of five participants per study arm was required. This threshold was selected to ensure adequate data for between-group comparison and to exclude very small case-series and aligns with current concerns regarding underpowered trials in sports and exercise science [ , ]. Moreover, the intervention was defined as a combined BCAA supplement (only leucine, isoleucine, and valine). In addition, studies had to include at least one group with a pure BCAA to observe isolated effects; thus, any studies with extra ingredients were excluded. The comparator was a matched control. The outcomes included were any measure of exercise performance and body composition. The study design was restricted to randomized controlled trials. Furthermore, any dissertations, theses, partially published articles, or articles with only abstracts were excluded. Data Collection and Analysis All records identified through database searches were imported into Covidence (Veritas Health Innovation, Melbourne, Australia) for screening. Duplicates were removed automatically within the platform and verified manually to ensure accuracy. Titles and abstracts were then screened to remove irrelevant studies, reviews, animal experiments, and non-randomized designs. Full texts of potentially eligible articles were retrieved and assessed against predefined inclusion and exclusion criteria. Any studies not meeting these criteria were excluded, and reasons for exclusion were documented (see Figure ). PRISMA flowchart indicating the distinct levels of screening, reasons for excluding articles and numbers of included/excluded articles at each stage PRISMA: Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses Risk of Bias Studies were assessed in RevMan 5 for selection bias, performance bias, detection bias, attrition bias, reporting bias and other biases (e.g. sponsors, uncontrolled variables) based on the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews [ ]. Studies were classified as unclear risk (if there was insufficient information to quantify risk), high risk (factors that could strongly influence objectivity of findings) or low risk (all reasonable information and controlled variables are clearly presented and accounted for). The Cochrane Handbook [ ] suggests that meta-analyses should not be performed when there is risk of bias in all/some studies; thus, as all RCTs included had instances of unclear/high risk, we chose not to perform a meta-analysis to avoid misleading results. Results Studies After the initial database search, 1973 articles were identified. Following removal of duplicates, 976 articles were screened by title and abstract. Of these, 909 articles were excluded as irrelevant, reviews, animal studies, or non-randomized trials; 67 full text articles were then assessed for eligibility, of which 45 were excluded for not meeting the inclusion criteria (e.g. mixed supplements, wrong population, or non-English/Portuguese text). Consequently, 22 studies met all inclusion criteria and were included in the final analysis (Figure ). The search covered all years up to 15 August 2025, and the publication years of included articles ranged from 2004 to 2025. Study durations varied from one day to six months. Of the 22 included studies, 21 were published in English and one in Portuguese. Participants The included studies were randomized controlled trials. A total of 511 people participated in these studies. The number of participants varied from 5 to 100 with a sample mean of 23.2 +/-19.2 participants. The ages varied from 18 to 71.7 years. Eighteen studies were done only on men, one was done only on women, and three included both women and men. Eleven studies included untrained participants (sedentary or healthy/active), whereas 11 included trained participants (athletes or “resistance-trained”) (see Table ). Seven-day washout, then crossover Risk of Bias Fifteen studies were classified as unclear bias risk due to giving insufficient information on randomization (Figure ). Sixteen studies did not offer information on allocation concealment. One study was single-blinded (labelled “high risk” as only participants were blinded), and two studies offered limited information on their double-blinded protocol (labelled “unclear risk”). Two did not blind their outcome assessors (high risk), whereas four other studies had unclear risk for detection bias due to insufficient information. One study omitted certain data (high risk of attrition bias), whereas one study gave no participant numbers or information on excluded data (unclear risk). A preliminary study protocol was not available for 15 studies; thus, they were classified as unclear risk of reporting bias. Two studies were labelled as high risk of other bias due to being funded by the supplement producer or failure to control protein intake. Multiple panels indicating our risk of bias assessment for the included studies (n=22) based on the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions. Green indicates low risk of bias, yellow indicates unclear risk, and red indicates high risk. Panel A shows an overall percentage of risk of bias per bias type. Panel B presents assessment of risk of bias for individual studies. Low risk was the most common level. Main Findings There was substantial heterogeneity amongst the studies regarding the parameters used to measure body composition, and performance (see Table ). The most common formulation of BCAAs was 2:1:1.
