Total knee arthroplasty HMB/Gln/Arg mixture (2.4/14/14 g per day) for 4 weeks Prevention of reduction of maximal strength of quadriceps muscle Nishizaki et al. Hip fracture HMB (3 g)/vitamin D/protein combination for 30 days Accelerated healing, shortening immobilization period, ↑ muscle strength Ekinci et al . Gastric bypass HMB/Gln/Arg mixture (1.5/7/7 g per day) for 8 weeks No benefits when compared with controls Clements et a l. Renal failure HMB (3 g/day) for 6 months No benefits Fitschen et al. Rheumatoid arthritis HMB/Gln/Arg mixture (3/14/14 g per day) for 12 weeks No benefits when compared with placebo Marcora et al . Experimental studies performed using in vitro models (mainly murine myotubes) and animal models of muscle wasting indicate that HMB may be effective in a number of disorders, notably in conditions of enhanced proteolysis in sepsis, cancer, immobilization, and steroid medication. Most of the studies demonstrated that the action of HMB is mediated by attenuating the proteasome activity and protein breakdown and not by stimulating protein synthesis ( Tables and ). In human studies, positive results were observed in chronic pulmonary disease, hip fracture, and in AIDS‐related and cancer‐related cachexia but not in rheumatoid cachexia, renal failure, and gastric bypass ( Table ). Unfortunately, these clinical studies frequently used mixed supplements that contained various components including glutamine, arginine, leucine, higher caloric or protein content, and vitamins. Therefore, it was not possible to determine which of the supplements was effective or if there was a synergistic effect. It should be noted that sarcopenia and/or cachexia is the most common complication of cirrhosis and adversely affects survival, quality of life, and the development of other complications. Considering that most of the HMB is synthesized from leucine in the liver and that the activation of leucine oxidation in skeletal muscle causes a decrease in the leucine level in the blood plasma of subjects with liver cirrhosis, , impaired HMB synthesis and its deficiency may be expected to develop in liver disease. However, to date, no animal or human studies of HMB supplementation in liver disease have been performed. Healthy subjects and effects of HMB consumption The HMB is one of the most frequently used supplements not only among athletes but also by people aiming to losses fat mass and prevent the age‐related loss of skeletal muscle. However, some studies indicate that the beneficial effects of HMB on skeletal muscle observed in subjects who exercise, elderly subjects and/or in muscle‐wasting conditions do not occur in healthy, non‐exercising subjects. Nissen and Abumrad reported in a study of non‐exercising women who received supplements of 3 g of HMB/day for 4 weeks that there were no changes in the body composition whereas in a similar study in which the women were subjected to resistance exercise, an increase in lean tissue and a decrease in fat mass were observed. In our study, the HMB counteracted the changes in the muscles of septic rats but had no effect in healthy animals. The explanation of the lack of favourable effects of HMB on muscle in healthy individuals who do not engage in a regular physical activity is not available. It seems that HMB modulates the balance between protein synthesis and proteolysis in a favour of anabolic reactions and that its requirement increases in conditions of accelerated turnover of muscle proteins that occurs in exercise and muscle‐wasting conditions. In this context, it should be noted that impaired insulin sensitivity has been demonstrated in healthy sedentary rats supplemented with HMB for 4 weeks. Dosage and drug forms A dose of 3 g of HMB per day is routinely recommended by manufacturer to maintain or improve muscle mass and function. This dose corresponds to intake of approximately 60 g of leucine. Consumption of such an amount of leucine should enhance the activity of the BCKD (the rate‐limiting enzyme of BCAA catabolism) and the oxidation of all the three BCAA, which would cause depletion of valine and isoleucine in body fluids. Such an imbalance in concentrations of the BCAA could exert adverse effects on protein metabolism in various tissues, and therefore, HMB supplementation cannot be replaced by leucine. Commercially, HMB is available as the calcium salt. Recently, administration of HMB in a free‐acid form of HMB in a gel has been investigated. Fuller et al. showed that administration of the free form of HMB resulted in more rapid and higher plasma concentrations and improved clearance of the HMB when compared with the calcium salt form and suggested that administration of HMB as free acid could improve HMB availability and efficacy to tissues. However, another study demonstrated higher bioavailability of HMB after administration of the calcium salt of HMB when compared with free‐acid form. Further studies are needed to determine the pros and cons of these two forms of HMB. Toxicity and adverse effects Several studies have demonstrated that supplemental HMB is well tolerated and has no toxic effects. , , However, although HMB is made naturally by the body, it is possible that the positive effects of HMB on the protein balance in muscle may exert some adverse effects in other tissues. Stimulation of protein synthesis and suppression of proteolysis by HMB decreases the release of various amino acids from muscles to the blood and may impair their availability in visceral tissues. Glutamine is of particular interest on the basis that it acts as an essential substrate for enterocytes and immune cells and its deficiency decreases protein synthesis in skeletal muscle. , The plasma glutamine concentration is already low in many patients with critical illness, and a decreased glutamine level has been reported after HMB treatment. Therefore, studies are needed to examine whether the positive effects of HMB on muscle mass in cachexia are associated with glutamine depletion and adverse effects in other tissues. Conclusions The reports summarized here indicate that HMB provides a number of benefits to subjects involved in strength‐power and endurance sports. The effects on muscle mass and strength, particularly during resistance training, are likely related to the suppression of proteolysis and a positive effect on protein synthesis. Its benefits in aerobic performance are probably more associated with improved mitochondrial biogenesis and fat oxidation. Favourable effects on the recovery from exercise‐induced damage may be related to the role of HMB as a precursor of cholesterol, which modulates membrane fluidity and affects ion channels, and membrane excitability. Studies have demonstrated that HMB can prevent the development of sarcopenia in elderly subjects and that the optimal action of HMB on muscle growth and strength occurs when it is combined with exercise. Unfortunately, exercise is performed only by a small percentage of elderly subjects. Several studies suggest that HMB supplementation is ineffective in healthy sedentary subjects. Studies performed under in vitro conditions and animal studies suggest that HMB may be effective as a treatment for muscle wasting in various forms of cachexia. However, clinical reports are rare, and most of them examined the therapeutic potential of combinations of various agents. It was therefore not possible to determine, which of the supplements was effective. Further studies examining the effects of HMB administered alone are needed to reach a conclusion regarding the specific effectiveness of HMB in attenuating muscle wasting in a range of catabolic conditions. Although most of the endogenous HMB is produced in the liver and impaired HMB production may be assumed to occur in liver disease, there are no reports regarding the metabolism of HMB and the effects of its supplementation in subjects with liver disease. Conflict of interest None declared.
journal: Journal of Cachexia, Sarcopenia and Muscle

title: Theoretical and Practical Aspects of Different Cluster Set Structures: A Systematic Review.
abstract: Tufano, JJ, Brown, LE, and Haff, GG. Theoretical and practical aspects of different cluster set structures: a systematic review. J Strength Cond Res 31(3): 848-867, 2017-When performing a set of successive repetitions, fatigue ensues and the quality of performance during subsequent repetitions contained in the set decreases. Oftentimes, this response may be beneficial because fatigue may stimulate the neuromuscular system to adapt, resulting in a super-compensatory response. However, there are instances in which accumulated fatigue may be detrimental to training or performance adaptations (i.e., power development). In these instances, the ability to recover and maintain repetition performance would be considered essential. By providing intermittent rest between individual repetitions or groups of repetitions within a set, an athlete is able to acutely alleviate fatigue, allowing performance to remain relatively constant throughout an exercise session. Within the scientific literature, a set that includes intermittent rest between individual repetitions or groups of repetitions within a set is defined as a cluster set. Recently, cluster sets (CS) have received more attention as researchers have begun to examine the acute and chronic responses to this relatively novel set structure. However, much of the rest period terminology within the literature lacks uniformity and many authors attempt to compare largely different protocols with the same terminology. Additionally, the present body of scientific literature has mainly focused on the effects of CS on power output, leaving the effects of CS on strength and hypertrophy relatively unexplored. Therefore, the purpose of this review was to further delineate cluster set terminology, describe the acute and chronic responses of CS, and explain the need for further investigation of the effects of CS.
journal: Journal of strength and conditioning research

title: Feasibility and safety of two weeks of blood flow restriction exercise in individuals with spinal cord injury
abstract: Context Reduced muscle strength and function are common after spinal cord injury (SCI). While low-load blood flow restriction exercise (BFRE) enhances muscle strength in healthy and clinical populations, its safety and feasibility in individuals with SCI remain underexplored. Objectives To investigate the feasibility and safety of low-load BFRE in individuals with incomplete SCI. Study Design Case series. Setting SCI rehabilitation center. Participants Six participants with motor incomplete SCI were enrolled in the study. Intervention A two-week BFRE intervention for the lower limbs was conducted twice weekly at 40% total arterial occlusion pressure, using 30-40% of 1-repetition maximum loads. Outcome Measures Feasibility measures, specifically recruitment and eligibility rates and intervention acceptability, were collected. Blood pressure (BP) responses and specific coagulation markers for deep vein thrombosis (DVT) were assessed as safety measures. Results Recruitment and eligibility rates were 2.8% and 6.6% for individuals admitted for first-time rehabilitation (subacute SCI) and 8.3% and 13.9% for 4-week readmission rehabilitation stays (chronic SCI), respectively. Intervention acceptability was high, characterized by 95.8% training adherence and low-to-moderate self-reported pain levels. BP responses and changes in C-reactive protein, Fibrinogen, and D-dimer during the intervention remained within clinically acceptable levels. Conclusion BFRE was feasible in an SCI rehabilitation setting despite a low recruitment rate. BFRE imposed no heightened risk of DVT or severe BP fluctuations in the present case series. Additionally, no severe adverse events occurred, and only mild complaints were reported. More extensive safety considerations warrant larger-scale exploration. Trial registration ClinicalTrials.gov identifier: NCT03690700 .
full_text: Introduction Spinal cord injury (SCI) can cause lifelong disability, reduced quality of life and decreased life expectancy ( ). Nonetheless, from 1990 to 2019, the incidence, prevalence and years of life lived with disability have increased substantially in SCI ( ). While the disease is complex and multifaceted, one important aspect of rehabilitation is to prevent or minimize physical deconditioning and increase physical function, as reduced physical activity entails an elevated risk of cardiovascular diseases and decreased quality of life ( ). To optimize recovery of motor performance, a combination of strength training and task-specific exercises is commonly applied during rehabilitation from SCI ( ). Traditional heavy-load (HL) resistance exercise (RE), typically performed at 60-80% of 1-repetition maximum (1RM), is considered the optimal regimen for enhancing maximal voluntary muscle strength (MVC) and muscle volume in healthy individuals ( ). However, increased fatigue, impaired postural balance, and skeletal muscle atrophy are some of the common consequences following SCI, resulting in reduced muscle strength and varying degrees of spasticity ( ). These factors can complicate the effects of HL-RE. Consequently, efficacious low-load (LL) exercise modalities are needed to enhance physical rehabilitation efforts in individuals with SCI. Blood flow restriction exercise (BFRE) involves RE combined with a pneumatic cuff applied proximally on the exercising limb(s). The cuff partially restricts blood circulation to and from the working limb muscle, inducing a hypoxic environment ( ). BFR is most commonly performed in the context of LL-RE (30-50% of 1RM) but can also be applied in conjunction with other forms of exercise modalities, such as walking, running or cycling ( ). The application of ischemia during the concurrent development of muscle tension appears to increase MVC and evoke hypertrophic adaptations in the trained musculature ( ). Previously, meta-analyses have demonstrated that LL-BFRE is superior to free-flow LL-RE in enhancing muscle strength and volume in healthy individuals ( , ), and showed comparable effects to HL-RE ( ). Additionally, previous studies suggest that combining BFRE with electrical stimulation may significantly increase muscle size in paralyzed muscles compared to using stimulation alone in individuals with SCI ( , ). The capacity of BFRE to induce muscle hypertrophy and strength gains despite the involvement of low-intensity muscle loadings makes it a potentially valuable exercise modality for SCI rehabilitation. However, the feasibility and safety of BFRE in individuals with SCI remain largely unknown, particularly regarding the tolerability of BFRE, recruitment rates, fluctuations in blood pressure (BP), and the risk of severe adverse events, among other factors. A systematic review by the authors examined the safety and efficacy of BFRE in individuals with neurological disorders and revealed that BFRE appears as a safe training modality for individuals with SCI ( ). However, this conclusion is based on a highly limited number of studies with small sample sizes, diverse study designs, and a moderate-to-high risk of bias. Therefore, individuals with SCI may present with an increased risk for adverse events (AEs) during exercise, such as deep vein thrombosis (DVT) or autonomic dysreflexia (AD). Factors such as sensory-motor deficits can pose challenges to the feasibility of BFRE interventions, particularly concerning cuff usage. Due to altered pain perception, individuals with SCI might not notice excessive cuff tightness or potential injuries underneath the cuff. This lack of sensory feedback may increase the risk of complications, such as pressure ulcers, and may also trigger AD episodes or elevate the risk of DVT due to potential excessive blood flow restriction, making BFRE interventions more challenging for this population. Additionally, the recruitment of individuals with SCI for clinical trials has previously been shown to be difficult, and low participation rates are one of the global challenges in SCI research ( ). However, to the best of our knowledge, this has not been investigated related to BFRE interventions before. Therefore, further studies are needed to investigate these aspects of BFRE in individuals with SCI. The aim of the present study was to investigate the feasibility and safety of a short-term LL-BFRE protocol in individuals with SCI. We hypothesized that the relatively conservative BFRE protocol used in this study – characterized by a low occlusion pressure, a fixed number of repetitions, and a training frequency of twice per week – would be both feasible and safe for individuals with SCI. Material & methods Setting and study population The present study was conducted in a SCI in-patient rehabilitation center in Denmark. The center is located in the western part of Denmark and covers a broad geographical area with a background population of ∼3.2 million individuals. Annually, the center admits an average of 90 individuals for long-term sub-acute rehabilitation following injury and 70 individuals for a 4-week follow-up rehabilitation during chronic SCI. The present study participants were recruited from May 2020 to June 2021. After obtaining informed consent, each participant's medical records were reviewed for eligibility by a specialist consultant neurologist. Inclusion criteria were: Age ≥ 18 years, exhibit a grade 2–4 on the Medical Research Council (MRC) Scale for Muscle Strength of the knee extensors, classification of grades A, B, C or D on the American Spinal Injury Association (ASIA) Impairment Scale (AIS) with a neurological level of injury below T6, and sufficient cognitive ability to follow instructions. Duration of SCI ≥ 1 month and <1 year (From May 2020 to February 2021). However, due to a poor recruitment rate, the original criteria for the duration of SCI were deemed not optimal, as elaborated in the results. Therefore, the duration of SCI was changed to ≥ 1 year for the rest of the study period (From March 2021 to June 2021). Exclusion criteria were: History of DVT and/or clinical episodes of AD, substance abuse, severe mental illness, uncontrolled hypertension, severe arteriosclerosis, coronary arterial disease, coagulopathy, collagen diseases, and severe neuropathies. All participants received detailed oral and written information before providing informed, written consent. After inclusion, medical history, neurological level of SCI, and demographic and anthropometric data were obtained through the electronic patient record. The study was approved by the Committee on Health Research Ethics (1-10-72-290-18) and was registered at ClinicalTrials.gov as a part of a larger study ( NCT03690700 ). Outcome assessment Feasibility Recruitment flow was reviewed by pre-screening all individuals with SCI admitted at the SCI rehabilitation center during the recruitment period. The purpose was to assess the proportion of admitted patients who would possibly be eligible to participate in the study and who consented to enrolment. Acceptability of the intervention was systematically monitored during the intervention period and included (i) adherence to the prescribed training regimen, (ii) evaluation of pain responses during training sessions and (iii) monitoring of AEs or general complaints. Adherence to the training protocol was assessed by recording whether participants attended all scheduled training sessions or had any absences. Subjective pain perception was assessed using the short-form McGill pain questionnaire five minutes before the initiation of each training session and immediately after the completion of every exercise. Hospital staff monitored participants for AEs on an ongoing basis, and study staff collected general complaints before each training session. Additionally, during all training sessions, participants were closely monitored for any adverse physiological responses, such as the development of pressure ulcers, dizziness, severe spasticity, fatigue, and abnormal or neuropathic pain responses. Following completion of the intervention, a custom-made questionnaire was utilized to assess participants’ satisfaction with the training protocol, including exercise volume, exercise selection, duration of a single training session, as well as whether the training and the occlusion bands caused discomfort. All questions had categorical reply options, as detailed in . Safety Monitoring for possible DVT formation involved assessing coagulation markers from venous blood samples drawn before (PRE: 30 minutes) and after (0-15 minutes (POST1) and 240 minutes (POST2)) the first and last training sessions. Markers of coagulation (fibrinogen and D-dimer) and inflammation (high-sensitivity C-reactive protein (CRP)) were analyzed at the Department of Biochemistry, Viborg Regional Hospital, Denmark, by an experienced biomedical scientist who was also responsible for retrieving the blood samples. Reference values were derived from collective decision-making for the clinical Region as well as existing literature ( , ). Continuous BP was monitored at every training session using a fingertip finometer (Finometer Midi, Finapres Medical Systems B.V., Amsterdam, The Netherlands) placed around the distal interphalangeal joint of the fourth finger on the right hand. Intervention Participants performed 45 min of LL-BFRE (30-40% 1RM) of the lower extremities twice/week for 2 weeks, consisting of 4 sets (30 × 15 × 15 × 15 repetitions, 45-sec pause between sets and 3 minutes pause between exercises) of unilateral seated leg extensions and hamstring leg curls. Resistance load was not adjusted during the study due to the short intervention period. To induce BFR, an 11-cm wide contour-shaped pneumatic occlusion cuff (Delfi Medical Innovations Inc., Canada) was placed proximally on the thigh close to the inguinal fold, using an occlusion pressure corresponding to 40% of seated arterial occlusion pressure (AOP). The computerized Delfi Tourniquet System (Delfi Medical Innovations Inc., Canada) was used to monitor and regulate the cuff pressure during exercise. The cuff remained inflated during all four sets (incl. pauses) while deflated between exercises. Determination of arterial occlusion pressure and resistance load As body position influences arterial occlusion pressure ( ), the individual's arterial occlusion pressure (AOP) was recorded at baseline while the participant was seated, and the tourniquet was positioned as described. Using Doppler ultrasound (Siemens ACUSON S2000TM), the pulsation of the A. Tibialis posterior was monitored distally to the medial malleolus. The tourniquet pressure was incrementally increased in series of 5 mmHg, starting from 100 mmHg, and the total AOP was determined when pulsations in A. Tibialis ceased ( , ). The cuff was immediately deflated after AOP was determined. The pressure of the tourniquet used during training was set to 40% of AOP. To determine the resistance load during BFRE, a 3RM test of unilateral leg extension and leg curl exercises and an RM calculation formula adjusted for sex and training state ( ) were conducted at baseline. The 3RM test, acknowledged for estimating maximal lifting capacity over three repetitions in untrained men and women ( ), involved a warm-up of 10 light-weight repetitions, followed by a one-minute rest. Subsequently, participants completed a set of 5 repetitions with an extra 10-20% of the initial weight. The 3RM attempt, with a 20-30% weight increase, typically was achieved in the third trial. All trials were separated by 3- to 5-minute rest periods. In successful trials, 10-20% weight was added, and for unsuccessful attempts, 5-10% was removed. No more than 5 trials, encompassing warm-ups, were allowed for 3RM determination. Statistical analysis Descriptive statistics of baseline characteristics, pain responses, satisfaction and discomfort with the protocol, coagulation markers and BP responses during the first raining session are presented individually for each participant. Study data were collected and managed using REDCap electronic data capture tools ( ) hosted at Aarhus University, Denmark. Figures were created in Microsoft Excel 2019 (Microsoft Corporation, Redmond, WA, USA). Results Six participants were recruited for the present study. The participants' characteristics are detailed in . Baseline characteristics. Participant Age Sex BMI AIS NLI Etiology (T/NT) TSI SCIM (0–100) tAOP mmHG (R / L) A 28 M 20.5 A Th8 T 3 months 93 175 / 165 B 52 F 27.8 D Th10 NT 3 months 95 200 / 210 C 30 M 21.8 D Th10 NT 1 month 84 175 / 165 D 22 M 20.7 D Th11 NT 22 years 98 175 / 185 E 21 F 20.3 D Th12 NT 21 years 95 185 / 175 F 81 M 29.8 D Th9 NT 14 years 65 185 / 200 AIS: American Spinal Injury Association Impairment Scale; BMI: Body Mass Index; L: Left; NLI: Neurological Level of Injury; NT, Non-traumatic; R: Right; SCIM: The Spinal Cord Independence Measure; tAOP: Total Arterial Occlusion Pressure; T, Traumatic; TSI: Time Since Injury Feasibility The recruitment process is presented in . During the period from May 2020 through February 2021, the recruitment rate and eligibility rate remained at 2.8% (3/106) and 6.6% (7/106) for first-time admissions, respectively, recruiting 1 participant every 3 months. This recruitment rate was deemed insufficient to sustain the original inclusion criteria regarding the duration of SCI. Consequently, modifications were implemented, requiring the injury duration to be ≥1 year and, therefore, recruiting exclusively from in-patients of the 4-week rehabilitation stay. The new inclusion criteria were evaluated during the period from March 2021 to June 2021, revealing an increased recruitment rate of 8.3% (3/36) and an eligibility rate of 13.9% (5/36). This adjustment allowed for the recruitment of ∼1 patient per month. No dropouts or withdrawals of consent occurred during the study. Participant A characterized the pain as a transient throbbing and cramping sensation in the thigh. Participant B reported a previously experienced issue of a burning sensation in the lower back. Participant D exhibited a single short-term incident of cramping and tenderness in the legs before the initiation of training. However, in all cases, the training sessions were completed as planned. Two participants reported complaints of a mild numbness sensation in the foot while the cuff was inflated, which abated immediately following cuff release. Additionally, one participant reported a high degree of knee extensor muscle fatigue during training session 1 of unilateral leg extension. This feeling resolved within 20 s of cuff deflation during the rest period, and therefore the training session continued as planned. Results from the satisfaction questionnaire are presented in . Overall, the results indicate an overall satisfaction with the training protocol. Participant A wanted to increase the training volume to 3–4 sessions per week, and participant B wished to include more overall leg exercises in the training.
